skill.name: svg-logo-generator
skill.description: Generate minimal geometric SVG logos with Python scripts, standard SVG primitives, fixed palettes, and scalable viewBox settings.
skill.summary: Create clean geometric SVG logos with simple Python generation workflows.
skill.author: Dwsy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command, network, weak crypto, and filesystem patterns, but review found the command, network, reconnaissance, and crypto alerts are false positives from Markdown backticks, SVG terms, XML namespace text, and design prose. The only confirmed risk is a benign Python example that writes a fixed local output file, which could overwrite logo.svg in the current directory.

low_findings.title: Local File Write to Fixed Output Path
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs generated Python scripts to save output as logo.svg and shows a direct write to that file. This is expected for a logo generator, but it can overwrite an existing file in the working directory if the user does not choose a unique path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write is explicit and local. The risk is limited because the path is relative, fixed, and tied to the declared output behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Command Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution alerts occur in Markdown inline code or a fenced Python example. No shell invocation, Ruby execution, subprocess call, or command interpolation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown formatting, SVG guidance, or Python code fences. There is no evidence of executable shell syntax beyond documentation backticks.

low_findings.title: Static Network and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URL is the standard SVG XML namespace, not a request target. The viewBox text is SVG layout guidance and does not perform network discovery or scanning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears only inside an SVG namespace attribute. No networking API, socket use, fetch call, or reconnaissance command exists in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The cited lines describe SVG logo generation, design analysis, color palettes, and color consistency. No cryptographic algorithms, hashing calls, password handling, or security-sensitive crypto usage are present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is design documentation and contains no crypto implementation. The scanner likely matched short substrings inside ordinary words or SVG-related prose.

dangerous_patterns.title: Python File Write Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a Python pattern that opens logo.svg for writing. This is a normal output mechanism, but users should avoid running it in directories where logo.svg must be preserved.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly writes to a relative local file. No deletion, traversal, privilege escalation, or external destination is present.

content.value_statement: Teams often need scalable logo concepts without heavy design tooling. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to create clean SVG logo files with Python and simple geometry.
actual_capabilities: Plans visual metaphors, color palettes, and geometric composition before generating code.
Uses standard SVG primitives such as circles, rectangles, paths, and polygons.
Creates self-contained Python scripts that generate a scalable SVG logo file.
Applies viewBox and XML namespace settings for responsive SVG output.
Encourages minimal dependencies by using Python standard library features.
limitations: It produces simple geometric concepts, not full brand systems or market-tested identities.
Generated logos may need manual refinement by a designer before commercial use.
The default workflow writes logo.svg and may overwrite that file if reused.
It does not verify trademark conflicts, licensing clearance, or brand uniqueness.

use_cases.title: Startup Logo Concept
use_cases.description: Create a first-pass geometric logo concept with a defined palette and scalable SVG output.

use_cases.title: Product Icon Draft
use_cases.description: Generate a simple product icon that developers can inspect, edit, and place in a prototype.

use_cases.title: Design System Placeholder
use_cases.description: Produce consistent placeholder marks for internal tools, demos, or early design explorations.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Logo Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a minimal SVG logo for a productivity app using two colors and simple geometric shapes.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a straightforward logo concept.

prompt_templates.title: Brand-Informed Logo
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a geometric SVG logo for a privacy-focused analytics product. Use a calm palette, a shield metaphor, and a 512 by 512 viewBox.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request with brand attributes and visual direction.

prompt_templates.title: Editable Python Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a self-contained Python script that creates an SVG logo for a developer tool. Use standard library features only and save the result as tool-logo.svg.
prompt_templates.scenario: Developer request for a reusable generation script.

prompt_templates.title: Refined Design System Mark
prompt_templates.prompt: Design three geometric SVG logo directions for a B2B workflow platform. Explain the metaphor, palette, and composition, then generate the strongest option as a Python-created SVG.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request that compares concepts before producing output.

output_examples.input: A logo for a note-taking app with a blue and green palette.
output_examples.output: A concise design rationale followed by a generated SVG file concept with layered rectangles, a folded-corner note shape, and a balanced two-color palette.

output_examples.input: A developer tool icon that feels precise and technical.
output_examples.output: A Python-generated SVG concept using a square grid, angular path shapes, and a restrained accent color for a scalable product icon.

output_examples.input: A privacy brand mark based on a shield and data points.
output_examples.output: A minimal shield composition with circular data nodes, clear palette notes, and a local SVG output suitable for review.

best_practices: Specify the brand purpose, target audience, color preferences, and desired visual metaphor.
Ask for a unique output filename when working in a directory that already contains logo files.
Review generated SVGs for accessibility, contrast, and brand fit before production use.
anti_patterns: Do not request imitation of an existing trademarked logo or brand identity.
Do not treat generated concepts as legally cleared brand assets.
Do not run generated file-writing scripts in a directory without checking output filenames.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates geometric SVG logo concepts and Python scripts that generate those SVG files.

faq.question: Does it require third-party Python packages?
faq.answer: No. The workflow recommends Python standard library features and direct SVG construction.

faq.question: Can I use the logo commercially?
faq.answer: You should review the design, check trademark conflicts, and confirm licensing before commercial use.

faq.question: Will it overwrite files?
faq.answer: The example writes logo.svg by default, so request a unique filename when needed.

faq.question: Does it generate raster images?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on SVG vector output, which can be scaled and edited.

faq.question: Can it make several concepts at once?
faq.answer: Yes. You can ask for multiple directions and then generate the strongest option.
